questions.question: Explain extendible hashing with the concepts of global depth, local depth, and directory. Demonstrate insertion of keys that causes directory doubling.
keyPoints: Directory is an array of 2^d pointers where d is the global depth
Global depth: number of hash bits used to index the directory
Local depth: number of hash bits distinguishing records in a specific bucket
When a bucket overflows and local depth == global depth, directory doubles
When local depth < global depth, only the bucket splits without directory growth
Demonstrate with a step-by-step insertion example

questions.question: Compare and contrast extendible hashing and linear hashing. Discuss their relative advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Extendible hashing uses a directory; linear hashing does not
Extendible hashing splits the overflowing bucket; linear hashing splits in round-robin order
Extendible hashing may double the directory (space overhead); linear hashing grows one bucket at a time
Linear hashing may have temporary overflow chains; extendible hashing avoids chains
Both achieve O(1) average lookup with at most one disk access for the bucket

questions.question: Explain linear hashing with the split pointer mechanism. How are hash functions h0 and h1 used during the splitting process?
keyPoints: Initial hash function h0(k) = k mod N where N is the initial number of buckets
Split pointer starts at bucket 0 and moves sequentially
When a split is triggered, bucket at split pointer is split using h1(k) = k mod 2N
Records from the split bucket are redistributed between the original and new bucket
After all buckets are split, a new round begins with h1 as the base function
